notes: Integrated from the real Grade 2 Quarter 2, Book 7 source file.
Early card: shorter text, short sentences, easiest independent start.

book.title: The Tale of Benjamin Bunny
book.description: The Tale of Benjamin Bunny includes a six-chapter reading sequence, vocabulary, comprehension questions, journal/writing prompts, and assessment checks.

choiceGuidance.reason: shorter text, short sentences, easiest independent start

weeks.title: The Tale of Benjamin Bunny Book Card
vocabulary: 1. mischievous - playful in a naughty way; likely to get into trouble
2. confident - sure of oneself; not afraid to try new things
1. persuasive - good at convincing others to do something
2. contentedly - in a happy, satisfied way
1. ridiculous - very silly or foolish looking
1. urgently - in a way that shows something is very important and needs immediate attention
2. desperately - in a way that shows you really, really need something
1. suspiciously - in a way that shows you think something is wrong
2. cramped - very tight and uncomfortable with no room to move
1. patiently - in a calm way, willing to wait without complaining
2. trespassing - going onto someone else's property without permission
journal: Write about a time curiosity led to a problem. How was the problem solved?
Compare Benjamin Bunny with another character from the story.
writing: Write about a time curiosity led to a problem. How was the problem solved?
Compare Benjamin Bunny with another character from the story.
questions: 1. What was missing when Benjamin saw Peter?
2. How was Benjamin different from Peter?
1. How did the rabbits get into the garden?
2. What did Benjamin do once he was in the garden?
1. Where did Peter find his jacket?
2. What else did Benjamin take besides Peter's clothes?
1. Who was coming back to the garden?
2. How did Benjamin's attitude change when he heard Mr. McGregor?
1. Where did the rabbits try to hide?
2. How did Mr. McGregor trap the rabbits?
1. Who came to rescue the trapped rabbits?
2. What did Benjamin's father do after the rescue?
assessment: Use chapter comprehension questions for daily checks.
Use journal/writing prompt as written-response evidence.
